question: config1 and config2 are different objects. Which pattern ensures a single shared instance?
explanation: Multiple AppConfig instances mean settings aren't shared — changing one doesn't affect the other. Singleton ensures exactly one instance exists, so all code reads the same configuration.

question: This Singleton has a subtle bug in multi-threaded apps. What is it?
options: Two threads can both see _instance as null and create two instances
explanation: If Thread A checks _instance == null and Thread B does the same before A finishes creating the instance, you get two Logger objects. Fix: use a lock, Lazy<T>, or static initializer for thread safety.

question: Why is Lazy<T> a good choice for Singleton?
options: It's thread-safe and only creates the instance when first accessed
It's required by C# for singletons
explanation: Lazy<T> guarantees thread-safe, lazy initialization out of the box. The instance is created only when .Value is first called, and .NET ensures only one thread creates it — no manual locking needed.

question: This Singleton has become a 'God Object'. What's the problem?
options: It holds unrelated state (score, audio, network) — violates SRP
explanation: GameManager manages score, players, config, audio, AND networking. That's too many responsibilities. Singleton is fine for one shared instance, but don't dump every global concern into it.

question: This lock-based Singleton works but has a performance issue. What is it?
options: Every access acquires the lock, even after instance exists
The lock object should be static
explanation: After the instance is created, the lock is unnecessary — but every call still acquires it. Double-checked locking (check null before AND inside the lock) or using Lazy<T> avoids this overhead.

question: Why is this test fragile?
options: TaxCalculator.Instance is a global dependency that can't be mocked
The test should use 200 instead of 100
explanation: OrderService secretly depends on TaxCalculator.Instance — a global singleton you can't replace in tests. If the real tax rate changes, the test breaks. Fix: inject ITaxCalculator instead of using a static Instance.

question: Is `??=` (null-coalescing assignment) thread-safe for Singleton?
options: No — two threads can race and create two instances
explanation: The ??= operator is NOT atomic. Two threads can evaluate _instance as null simultaneously and both create new instances. For thread-safe Singleton, use Lazy<T> or a static initializer.

question: Why does a print spooler make sense as a Singleton?
options: All print jobs should go through one shared queue to prevent conflicts
Queues must be singletons in C#
PrintJob is too expensive to create
It's faster than a regular class
explanation: If multiple spooler instances existed, print jobs would be split across different queues, causing chaos. One shared spooler ensures all jobs are ordered and processed sequentially — Singleton is the right fit.

question: What happens if LoadFromFile() throws during Singleton creation?
options: The Singleton is never created and every access throws
It creates the instance with empty flags
explanation: With a static initializer, if the constructor throws, the type is marked as failed. Every subsequent access to Instance throws a TypeInitializationException. There's no retry — the Singleton is permanently broken.

question: Is this DI-based singleton better than a static Instance property?
options: Yes — it's testable (inject mock ICache) and swappable
Yes — but only because it uses Redis
No — AddSingleton and static Instance are identical
explanation: DI-managed singletons give you one instance (like static) PLUS testability (inject mocks), swappability (change implementation in one place), and proper lifecycle management. Best of both worlds.

question: Which pattern removes the if/else chain for creating senders?
explanation: Factory Method lets each notification type create its own sender through a method override. The client code works with the abstract creator — no if/else needed to pick the right sender.

question: Where is the Factory Method in this code?
options: CreatePage() — subclasses decide which page type to create
AddPage() — it adds pages to the list
explanation: CreatePage() is the factory method — it's abstract in the base class and each subclass overrides it to create the appropriate page type. The base class calls it without knowing which concrete page is created.

question: Is this a true Factory Method pattern?
options: No — this is a Simple Factory (static method, no inheritance)
Yes — it creates objects based on input
No — it should return an interface
Yes — the switch is the factory method
explanation: This is a Simple Factory — a static helper that centralizes creation logic. True Factory Method uses inheritance: a base class defines an abstract creation method, subclasses override it. Different patterns!

question: What's the main benefit of this Factory Method design?
options: Adding a new level with new enemies doesn't change existing code
Wolf and Scorpion share the same code
explanation: Want an OceanLevel with Sharks? Just create a new subclass — ForestLevel and DesertLevel remain untouched. The base class Initialize() works with any enemy through the factory method.

question: To properly use Factory Method, what should you change?
options: Make ButtonFactory abstract with CreateButton as an abstract method, one subclass per OS
Return a concrete Button instead of IButton
explanation: True Factory Method: abstract ButtonFactory with abstract CreateButton(). Then WindowsButtonFactory, MacButtonFactory, LinuxButtonFactory each override it. No if/else, and each factory can be extended independently.

question: Why does LoggerFactory define both CreateLogger AND Log?
options: Log uses CreateLogger — the factory method provides the object, base class uses it
explanation: This is the Template Method + Factory Method combo. The base class defines the algorithm (Log), and delegates object creation to subclasses via CreateLogger(). The base class doesn't know or care which logger it gets.

question: The client directly creates `new Truck()`. How does Factory Method help?
options: A factory decides which transport to create — client doesn't pick
Ship and Truck should merge into one class
explanation: With Factory Method, a LogisticsFactory subclass (RoadLogistics, SeaLogistics) creates the right transport. The client calls factory.CreateTransport() — it doesn't know or care if it gets a Truck or Ship.

question: Adding MongoDB support requires modifying this switch. Which principles does this violate?
options: Open/Closed Principle — and Factory Method pattern would fix it
No principles — switch is fine here
explanation: Every new database type means changing this class. Factory Method fixes it: abstract ConnectionFactory with CreateConnection(), and MySqlFactory, PostgresFactory each override it. New DB? New factory class, no changes to existing code.

question: What pattern does the Render method demonstrate alongside Factory Method?
options: Template Method — defines the algorithm, delegates creation to subclass
explanation: Render() is a template method: it defines the steps (create button, set click, render) but lets subclasses decide WHICH button via CreateButton(). Factory Method is often used inside Template Method.

question: Refactoring this with Factory Method — what becomes the factory method?
options: An abstract CreatePayment(Order) method in a base PaymentProcessor
explanation: Define abstract PaymentProcessor with abstract IPayment CreatePayment(Order order). USPaymentProcessor returns StripePayment, IndiaPaymentProcessor returns RazorpayPayment. Process() calls CreatePayment() — no if/else.

question: Which pattern creates families of related objects (button + textbox + checkbox) together?
explanation: Abstract Factory creates entire families of related objects. A WindowsUIFactory creates WindowsButton, WindowsTextBox, WindowsCheckbox together. A MacUIFactory creates the Mac versions. The client code works with the factory interface — never picks individual concrete types.

question: What's the key advantage over individual Factory Methods?
options: It guarantees consistency — Dark button always pairs with Dark textbox
explanation: Abstract Factory ensures you never accidentally mix a DarkButton with a LightTextBox. The factory creates the whole family together, guaranteeing visual consistency across all UI components.

question: What's the problem if you add a 'High Contrast' theme?
options: You must add another if-branch and touch App — Abstract Factory avoids this
DarkButton needs to support high contrast
You need a new App class
explanation: Every new theme means modifying App. With Abstract Factory: create HighContrastFactory implementing IUIFactory. Pass it to App. Zero changes to App — it just calls factory.CreateButton() and factory.CreateTextBox().

question: Why is this better than creating each database object separately?
options: SqlServer connection + Postgres command would crash — factory prevents mixing
It's faster than creating objects individually
IDbFactory is simpler than three interfaces
explanation: A SQL Server connection can't work with a Postgres command — they're incompatible. Abstract Factory ensures all three objects come from the same database family, preventing runtime errors from mixed types.

question: Adding an 'Underwater' biome requires what?
options: A new UnderwaterFactory implementing IEnvironmentFactory — GameWorld unchanged
Modifying GameWorld to handle underwater logic
Changing the terrain, sky, and music interfaces
explanation: Just create UnderwaterFactory with CreateTerrain → CoralReef, CreateSky → OceanSurface, CreateMusic → AmbientBubbles. Pass it to GameWorld — zero changes to existing code.

question: This creates multiple related objects from one factory. What pattern is this?
options: Abstract Factory — creating a family of platform-specific UI components
explanation: One factory creating multiple related objects (button, menu, scrollbar) that all belong to the same platform family — that's Abstract Factory. Factory Method would handle just one product type.

question: A customer wants a VictorianChair with a ModernTable. Should the factory allow this?
options: No — Abstract Factory prevents mixing styles to ensure consistency
Yes — the customer should choose freely
explanation: The whole point of Abstract Factory is preventing inconsistent combinations. Victorian furniture has ornate curves; Modern is minimalist. Mixing them creates visual chaos. If mixing is needed, it's a different pattern.

question: How does Abstract Factory differ from Builder here?
options: Factory creates independent objects; Builder constructs one complex object step by step
They're the same pattern with different names
explanation: Abstract Factory returns separate, independent products (drink, side, main). Builder would construct ONE Meal object step by step: meal.SetDrink().SetSide().SetMain().Build(). Different intents.

question: Refactoring with Abstract Factory — what becomes the factory?
explanation: Define IExportFactory with CreateHeader(), CreateBody(), CreateFooter(). PdfExportFactory and HtmlExportFactory implement it. DocumentExporter takes a factory — no if/else, and you can add Word, Markdown, etc.

question: The platform check happens only once at startup. Why is this important?
options: All UI components are guaranteed to be from the same platform family
It's faster than checking every time
The factory can't change after creation
explanation: By choosing the factory once, every component created throughout the app comes from the same family. No risk of a Windows button appearing in a Mac dialog — consistency is enforced by design.

question: This constructor has 13 parameters. Which pattern makes this readable?
options: Builder — construct step by step with named methods
explanation: Builder lets you write: new UserBuilder().WithName("John", "Doe").WithEmail("john@email.com").InCity("Springfield").Build(). Each step is named and clear — no guessing what the 7th parameter means.

code:
var query = new SqlQueryBuilder()
  .Select("name", "email")
  .From("users")
  .Where("age > 18")
  .OrderBy("name")
  .Limit(10)
  .Build();
question: What makes this a Builder pattern?
options: Step-by-step construction with a final Build() that produces the result
explanation: Builder constructs a complex object (SQL query) step by step. Each method adds a part, and Build() assembles the final product. Method chaining is just a convenience — the pattern is about incremental construction.

question: How does Builder prevent incomplete Pizza objects?
options: Build() can validate all required fields before returning the object
Builder throws if Cheese is null
explanation: With a Builder, the Build() method can check that dough, sauce, and cheese are all set before creating the Pizza. Direct property setting has no validation — you get a cheeseless pizza with no warning.

question: Why does each method `return this`?
options: To enable fluent method chaining: builder.SetFloors(2).AddGarage().Build()
It's required by the Builder pattern
explanation: Returning 'this' enables fluent chaining — you can call multiple methods in one expression. It's not required by the pattern, but it makes Builder code much more readable and natural to write.

question: What's the biggest readability win with Builder?
options: No positional nulls — you see what's set and what's intentionally skipped
explanation: In the constructor call, what are those two 'null' values at the end? Nobody knows without checking the parameter list. Builder makes every value self-documenting and skipping optional fields is simply not calling that method.

question: How does having multiple builders help?
options: Same construction steps produce different representations (PDF vs HTML)
explanation: The same director can call SetTitle, AddChart, AddTable on either builder. PdfReportBuilder produces a PDF file; HtmlReportBuilder produces a web page. Same process, different output formats.

question: What's useful about the default values for _server and _port?
options: You only need to set what differs from defaults — most configs just set Database
explanation: Builder with sensible defaults means: new ConnectionStringBuilder().Database("mydb").Build() gives you a working connection string. You override only what you need — localhost:5432 works for most dev setups.

question: Why should the built object be immutable?
options: Once configured and validated by Build(), it shouldn't be changed unexpectedly
explanation: The Builder validates and constructs a consistent configuration. If someone could change MaxRetries after Build(), they'd bypass that validation. Immutability guarantees the built object stays in a valid state.

question: What role does the Director play?
options: It defines preset construction recipes — which steps in what order
explanation: The Director encapsulates construction recipes. BuildSportsCar and BuildSUV are presets — the Director knows the steps, the Builder knows how to execute them. Clients can use the Director for common configs or use the Builder directly.

question: Header() is called twice with different values. What does this demonstrate?
options: Builder supports repeated/accumulative steps — adding multiple headers
It's a bug — Header should only be called once
explanation: Builder can accumulate values — each Header() call adds to a collection. This is something constructors can't do elegantly. You can call AddTopping() five times on a PizzaBuilder the same way.

question: Which pattern avoids repeating setup for each new report?
options: Prototype — clone the template instead of rebuilding
explanation: Prototype lets you clone the template: var report = template.Clone(). All properties are copied, including the expensive logo. Then customize just what differs per report — no redundant setup.

question: The copy changed the original's address. What went wrong?
options: It's a reference copy, not a clone — both point to the same Address object
explanation: Assignment copies the reference, not the object. Both 'original' and 'copy' point to the same User and the same Address. Prototype pattern uses deep cloning to create truly independent copies.

question: MemberwiseClone creates a shallow copy. When is this NOT enough?
options: When the object has reference-type fields (like a list or nested object)
When the object has many properties
explanation: MemberwiseClone copies value types (int, string) correctly, but reference-type fields (lists, nested objects) still share the same reference. If Shape had a List<Point> corners, both copies would share the same list.

question: Why does DeepClone create a new list of cloned pages?
options: So modifying the clone's pages doesn't affect the original
explanation: Without deep cloning the Pages list, adding or editing a page in the clone would also change the original document. Deep clone creates independent copies of all nested reference types.

question: What's the benefit of a prototype registry?
options: Pre-configured prototypes can be cloned by name — no setup code needed
It limits the number of clones
explanation: Register a fully configured 'default-report' prototype once. Later, Create("default-report") gives you a clone with all settings pre-applied. No need to remember or repeat the setup every time.

question: After cloning, cell2 has a different formula but same format. Is cell2.Format independent?
options: Only if Clone() deep-copies the Format object
Yes — Clone() always creates independent copies
It depends on whether Format is a struct
explanation: If Clone() uses MemberwiseClone, cell2.Format points to the same CellFormat object. Changing cell2.Format.Bold would also change cell1's format. Deep cloning the Format is needed for independence.

question: Why clone instead of creating 500 new Trees from scratch?
options: Loading mesh and texture is expensive — clone reuses the loaded data
explanation: LoadMesh and LoadTexture are heavy I/O operations. Cloning copies the already-loaded mesh and texture data — each tree gets the same visuals without re-reading files 500 times.

question: The Loot list is copied with `new List<Item>(Loot)`. Is this deep enough?
options: Only if Item is immutable — otherwise Item objects are still shared
Yes — creating a new list is always deep enough
It depends on the list size
explanation: new List<Item>(Loot) creates a new list but the Item references inside are shared. If an Item has mutable properties, changing loot in the clone affects the original. If Items are immutable (read-only), this is fine.

question: Using serialization for cloning — what's the trade-off?
options: Always deep copies everything, but slower and needs serializable types
It doesn't work with nested objects
explanation: Serialize → deserialize creates a completely independent deep copy automatically. The downside: it's much slower than MemberwiseClone, and all types must be serializable. Great for prototyping, not for hot paths.

question: Why is Prototype the right pattern here instead of Builder?
options: Most properties are identical — only the name changes per user
Notification is too simple for Builder
explanation: When 90% of the object is the same and you're just tweaking small details per instance, cloning is more efficient and readable than rebuilding from scratch with a Builder each time.
